"And no parents," Jenna added, pouring a cup of Fresh-Breeze detergent into the machine. "Just us."

Sarah started the washer. KA-CHUNK. WHOOSH. The water began to fill, and the heavy agitator began to churn. The noise was significant—a rhythmic, mechanical thrum that echoed off the cinder blocks.

"Okay," Sarah said, sitting on an overturned milk crate. "First load is running. Now we wait. And fold."

Ten minutes passed. Jenna was pacing. She had finished a can of Citrus-Pop on the drive over and had just guzzled a bottle of water.

She stopped pacing. She did a little hop.

"Ugh," Jenna groaned. "My bladder is full."

She looked at the wooden stairs. They looked steep. Then she looked at the ceiling, imagining the hallway bathroom.

"Freddie said the upstairs bathroom is a 'bottleneck,'" Jenna noted. "And I don't feel like walking up there just to find out Aunt Linda is in there doing her hair."

Jenna turned and looked at the corner.

Harper froze. She watched Jenna look at the Pittsburgh Toilet.

"Jenna, don't," Harper whispered. "It's... out in the open."

"So?" Jenna shrugged. "We're all sisters. Besides, listen to that washer."

THUNKA-THUNKA-WHOOSH.

"It's loud," Jenna pointed out. "It's basically a sound barrier. Sonic privacy."

Jenna made a decision. She walked over to the toilet.

"Turn around, troops," Jenna commanded, waving her hand. "Eyes on the laundry."

Sarah calmly turned her back, picking up a flannel shirt to inspect a button. Harper hesitated, eyes wide, then quickly spun around to face the dryer.

"I am using the asset," Jenna announced dramatically.

Harper heard the rustle of denim. She heard the seat creak.

She waited for the shame. She waited for the awkwardness to suffocate the room.

But nothing happened. Sarah was humming to herself. The washer was roaring. Jenna was just... peeing.

"You know," Jenna called out casually from the corner, her voice echoing slightly. "This is actually kind of efficient. No doors to open. No waiting. It's a power move."

Harper blinked. Jenna wasn't dying of embarrassment. She was making a review.

"Flush incoming!" Jenna warned.

KA-WHOOSH.

The pipes banged, shaking the rafters.

Jenna walked back to the group, adjusting her jeans. She washed her hands in the utility sink with the bar of harsh yellow soap.

"See?" Jenna said, flicking water at Harper. "I survived. No lightning struck me."

Sarah looked at Harper over the rim of her glasses.

"It is a toilet, Harper," Sarah said, her voice gentle but practical. "It is a tool. If you have to go, you go. It doesn't matter if there are walls or not. Biology doesn't care about architecture."

"And honestly," Jenna added, sitting back down on a pile of dirty clothes. "Whatever happened last night? Forget it. If I'm willing to use it in broad daylight, you don't need to worry about using it in the dark."

Harper looked at the toilet. It didn't look like a monster anymore. It just looked like a toilet.

"Okay," Harper whispered. She relaxed her shoulders.

"Good," Sarah said. She tossed a pair of socks at Jenna. "Now get back to work. Pile C isn't going to sort itself."

The Laundry Brigade resumed operations. The washer churned, the dryer hummed, and the Pittsburgh Toilet sat in the corner—no longer a throne of shame, just another part of the plumbing.

### Part 10: The Anchor Point

#### 10:15 AM The Living Room

Upstairs, the house was quiet, save for the muffled rumble of the washer below the floorboards.

Freddie sat on the sofa. He was completely surrounded.

- Port Side: Paige was glued to him. She was reading a book, but her head was on his shoulder, and her hand was holding his hand, tracing the lines of his palm.

- Starboard Side: Amanda sat cross-legged next to him. She was drawing in a sketchbook, occasionally resting her head on his knee to check in.

- Forward Position: Elara sat on the floor, leaning back against his legs, reading a comic book.

Freddie felt the weight of them. It was heavy. It was hot.

"Status?" Paige whispered, turning a page with her free hand.

"Grounding is at 100%," Freddie reported. "The signal is clear."

He looked at Paige. He saw the tension in her neck.

"You are guarding me," Freddie stated softly.

Paige didn't look up from her book. "She tried to get in, Freddie. She tried to take you."

"She failed," Freddie reminded her. "The math won."

"I know," Paige said. She looked up then, her eyes fierce. "But she made you doubt. She made you think you were broken. I felt it, Freddie. I felt your panic. I am not letting that happen again."

She squeezed his hand so hard it almost hurt.

"You are the Core," Paige whispered. "If the Core crashes, the whole system goes down. I am the firewall. No one touches you unless they have clearance."

Freddie looked at Amanda drawing on his left. He looked at Elara leaning on his legs. He looked at Paige.

"You have clearance," Freddie said. "All of you."

"Yeah," Paige softened, resting her head back on his shoulder. "We do."

Amanda looked up from her drawing. She saw the serious look on their faces. She put her pencil down.

She stood up on the sofa cushions. She climbed onto Freddie’s lap, careful not to hurt his legs. She wrapped her arms around his neck and buried her face in his shirt.

"Anchoring," Elara narrated from the floor.

Freddie wrapped his free arm around Amanda. He held Paige’s hand with the other.

"Anchoring received," Freddie whispered.

Downstairs, the washing machine hit the spin cycle, vibrating the floor. Upstairs, the Avery Unit held onto each other, refusing to let go, proving that the strongest gravity wasn't physics—it was family.

### Part 11: The Zero-Privacy Protocol

#### Sunday, June 20, 2004 — 12:30 PM The Underworld (Basement)

Two hours had passed. The pile of dirty clothes had shrunk, replaced by neat, geometric stacks of folded laundry on the folding table Sarah had set up.

The basement air was thick with the scent of Fresh-Breeze detergent and warm lint. The Titan-Wash machine was grinding through Load 3 (Heavy Denims), while the dryer tumbled Load 2 (Delicates).

Sarah wiped sweat from her forehead. "Status check," she announced, folding a pair of jeans with a sharp snap. "We are at 60% completion. The denim cycle is taking longer than anticipated."

"It’s because Freddie wears those heavy carpenter jeans," Jenna noted, tossing a pair of socks into the 'orphan' pile. "They weigh a ton when they're wet."

Jenna reached for another can of Citrus-Pop. She cracked the tab. Hiss-snap.

"You know," Jenna said, taking a sip. "All this liquid input equals output."

She stood up. She didn't announce it dramatically this time. She just walked over to the corner.

Harper didn't flinch. She didn't turn around. She just kept matching socks.

"Turn up the radio," Jenna called out casually over her shoulder. "I don't need a soundtrack."

Sarah reached over and turned up the volume on the small boombox they had brought down. A pop song filled the basement, masking the sound.

Jenna used the Pittsburgh Toilet. She washed her hands. She came back.

"Next load," Jenna said, sitting back down as if she had just fetched a glass of water.

Ten minutes later, Sarah stood up. She dusted off her knees.

"My turn," Sarah stated pragmatically. "I’m not walking up two flights of stairs just to wait for Aunt Linda."

Sarah—the Lieutenant, the one who enforced the rules—walked to the corner. She didn't ask anyone to turn around. She trusted her squad.

Harper watched her out of the corner of her eye. She saw Sarah unbutton her jeans, sit down, and stare blankly at the furnace while she did her business. Sarah looked bored. She looked completely unbothered.

It really isn't a big deal, Harper realized. It’s just us.

A few minutes later, the urge hit Harper again. The basement was cold, but the dryer made it warm, and the combination always made her have to go.

She stood up.

"I have to use the asset," Harper said, testing the words.

"Go for it," Jenna said, not looking up from a t-shirt she was folding. "Don't forget to flush."

Harper walked to the corner.

She lowered her pants. She sat on the cold porcelain.

She looked at her sisters. Jenna was tossing a shirt to Sarah. Sarah was stacking it. They were ten feet away. They could see her.

And they didn't care.

Harper relaxed. She let it go.

For the first time in her life, she felt a total absence of social anxiety. In the outside world—at school, at the mall—privacy was a fortress you had to defend. You locked doors. You checked for gaps in the stall. You died of shame if someone heard a noise.

But here, in the Avery Underworld, the walls were down.

We don't hide anything, Harper thought. We don't hide our bodies. We don't hide our problems. We don't hide Freddie.

She stood up, pulled up her pants, and flushed.

KA-WHOOSH.

The roar of the water was the sound of victory.

Harper washed her hands and walked back to the group. She sat down on the concrete.

"You know," Harper said, picking up a pair of Freddie’s socks. "I think I like this toilet. It’s honest."

"It’s efficient," Sarah corrected, handing her another sock. "Now hurry up. The dryer is buzzing. Load 3 is ready for transfer."

The Laundry Brigade worked on. They rotated through the loads, and they rotated through the corner, bound together by soap, warm flannel, and the absolute freedom of having nothing left to hide.

### Part 12: Structural Integrity Test

#### Sunday, June 20, 2004 — 12:30 PM The Living Room (Surface Level)

While the Laundry Brigade conquered the mountains of denim in the basement, the living room had been transformed into a high-density affection zone.

Freddie sat in the center of the beige sofa. To an outside observer, he might have looked like a hostage. To Freddie, he felt like a tree that had been colonized by very aggressive, very loving wildlife.

Paige was the root system. She hadn't moved in an hour. She was pressed tight against his left side, her arm locked through his, her head resting heavily on his shoulder. She was reading a paperback with one hand, her thumb rhythmically stroking the side of his arm. She was the static variable—the unmoving anchor that kept him from floating away.

The twins, Amanda and Elara, were the dynamic variables.

"Scaling the north face!" Elara announced.

Elara (Age 9) had climbed up the back of the sofa. She draped herself over Freddie’s shoulders like a human scarf, her chin resting on top of his head, her arms dangling loosely over his chest.

"Elara," Freddie noted, his voice vibrating through her chest. "You are obstructing my visual field."

"I'm a hat," Elara giggled, swinging her legs which were dangling behind his head. "I'm keeping your brain warm."

Amanda (Age 9) occupied the southern territory. She was kneeling on the sofa cushions on Freddie’s right side. She wasn't content to just sit next to him; she treated him like a piece of playground equipment.

She climbed carefully onto his lap, avoiding his sensitive knees with practiced precision. She straddled his waist, facing him, and wrapped her arms around his neck. She pressed her forehead against his nose—her signature "Mind Meld."

Input Warning: Restriction of movement. Temperature critical. Oxygen intake reduced by 15%.

Freddie’s logic brain flashed the red lights. He was completely immobilized. He had a sister on his left, a sister on his head, and a sister on his chest. He couldn't scratch his nose if he wanted to.

He closed his eyes. He took a breath, smelling the vanilla shampoo Elara used and the faint scent of crayons on Amanda’s hands.

He compared this to the Pathfinder bus. He compared it to the empty apartment in Norfolk. He compared it to the cold, clinical way Vanessa had touched him—like he was a project to be fixed, not a person to be held.

This wasn't fixing. This was fusing.

"Status?" Paige asked softly, feeling his breathing shift under the weight of the twins. "Are they crushing you?"

"Structural integrity is holding," Freddie whispered. "The pressure is... acceptable."

Amanda pulled back from the Mind Meld. She looked at Freddie with wide, dark eyes. She took his glasses off his face and put them on her own. They were way too big; they slid down her nose instantly.

She smiled—a silent, gummy grin.

"She says you look like a bug with them on," Elara translated from the top of his head. "And she says you are squishy."

"I am not squishy," Freddie corrected, gently taking his glasses back from Amanda and sliding them back on. "I am resilient."

Amanda hummed a low note—Mmm-hmm—and collapsed forward, burying her face in the crook of his neck. She went boneless, turning into dead weight.

"Docking complete," Freddie murmured, wrapping his free arm around Amanda’s back to keep her from sliding off.

"I want in," Elara decided.

She slid down from the top of his head, tumbling over his shoulder like a slinky, and wedged herself into the tiny gap between Freddie and the armrest on the right. She grabbed Freddie’s right hand and started playing with his fingers, counting them one by one.

"One, two, three, four, thumb," Elara counted.

Freddie sat there. He was a lattice of limbs. He was the center of their universe.

"You know," Paige said, not looking up from her book, but squeezing his arm tighter. "Vanessa would hate this."

"She would categorize it as chaotic," Freddie agreed. "She would say it lacks boundaries."

"Do we have boundaries?" Elara asked, interlocking her fingers with Freddie’s.

"Negative," Freddie said. "We have connections."

He rested his chin on top of Amanda’s head. He leaned his cheek against Elara’s hair. He felt Paige breathing against his ribs.

The sensory overload was massive. It was loud, hot, and heavy.

And Freddie wouldn't have traded it for all the silence in the world.

"Hold position," Freddie ordered softly.

"Aye aye, Captain," Elara whispered.

Amanda just squeezed him tighter.

The Jungle Gym was open, and it was never closing down.

### Part 13: The Rotation of the Guard

#### Sunday, June 20, 2004 — 14:00 PM The Living Room (Surface Level)

The front door opened with a rattle of keys. Jessica and Aunt Linda backed into the entryway, arms laden with plastic bags from the party store and the grocery market.

"Mission successful," Jessica announced, kicking the door shut with her heel. "We have enough sugar to kill a horse and... oh."

She stopped. Aunt Linda stopped beside her.

They looked at the living room.

It was a sculpture of human entanglement. Freddie was barely visible. He was the buried foundation of a structure built entirely of sisters.

- Paige was the immovable wall on his left, her arm locked through his, her eyes darting up to check the door before relaxing when she saw it was just them.

- Amanda was draped over his lap, asleep, her face pressed into his neck.

- Elara was perched on the back of the sofa, her arms wrapped around his forehead, her chin resting on his hair.

Aunt Linda set her bags down quietly. She watched them for a moment.

She knew what her sister, Ellen, would say. Ellen would purse her lips and talk about "propriety." She would say it was "unseemly" for a nineteen-year-old man to be cuddling with his sisters like a litter of puppies. She would worry about what the neighbors thought.

She knew what Robert would say. He would clear his throat, uncomfortable with the emotion, and tell Freddie to "man up" and stop being a pillow.

And she knew what that girl Vanessa would say. She would call it "codependent." She would say it was "sick." She would try to cut the wires one by one to isolate the power source.

Linda looked at Freddie’s face. His eyes were closed. He wasn't flinching. He wasn't grey and shaking like he had been after the divorce. He looked peaceful. He looked anchored.

Screw them, Linda thought fiercely. This isn't sick. This is survival.

Just then, the basement door creaked open.

The Laundry Brigade emerged. Sarah, Jenna, and Harper walked into the hallway, carrying baskets of warm, folded clothes. They smelled like Fresh-Breeze and victory.

They stopped. They looked at the sofa. They looked at the monopoly the twins held on Freddie.

Jenna put her hands on her hips. "Hey! We’ve been slaving away in the dungeon for three hours! We want in."

Elara tightened her grip on Freddie’s head. "Squatter's rights!"

"Negative," Aunt Linda stepped in, her voice firm but kind. "We operate on a fair-trade policy in this house."

She walked over to the sofa. She reached out and gently poked Elara in the ribs.

"Okay, monkeys. Detach. You've had him all morning. The Laundry Brigade needs to recharge."

Amanda stirred, blinking sleepily. She looked at Linda, then at Harper standing by the laundry basket with a hopeful look on her face.

Amanda sighed—a long, dramatic exhale through her nose—and untangled herself from Freddie’s neck. She climbed off his lap, patted his knee twice (a thank-you), and slid to the floor.

Elara groaned but slid down from the back of the sofa. "Fine. But I'm sitting next to Jessica."

"Deal," Jessica said, dropping onto the love seat.

The path was clear. Paige, of course, did not move. She shifted slightly to make room, but her grip on Freddie’s left arm didn't loosen by a millimeter. She was the permanent fixture.

"Rotation initiated," Freddie announced softly, adjusting his glasses which Elara had knocked crooked.

Harper moved first. She needed this. After the shame of the Pittsburgh Toilet and the cold basement, she needed to know she was still welcome in the warm spot.

She walked over and sat exactly where Amanda had been—right on Freddie’s lap. She was heavy, leggy, and awkward, but Freddie wrapped his right arm around her instantly, pulling her against his chest.

"Welcome back, Specialist," Freddie whispered into her hair.

Sarah sat on the coffee table in front of him, leaning forward to rest her forehead against his knee. "Laundry is at 100%. Socks are matched."

"Efficiency noted," Freddie said, reaching out to tap her shoulder.

Jenna flopped onto the sofa on his right side, wedging herself into the gap Elara had vacated. She grabbed Freddie’s hand and threw her leg over his shins.

"We conquered the toilet, Freddie," Jenna bragged, closing her eyes. "We are warriors."

"You are a formidable unit," Freddie agreed.

Linda watched the pile reform. The faces changed, but the structure remained the same. Freddie was the core, and his sisters were the shielding.

"Okay," Linda whispered to Jessica, picking up the grocery bags again. "Let's get this ice cream in the freezer before it melts. I think the army is occupied for a while."

"Yeah," Jessica smiled, looking at her siblings. "They're good."

Linda looked one last time at Freddie, buried once again under a fresh set of sisters, looking happier than she had ever seen him.

Eat your heart out, Vanessa, Linda thought with a grim sense of satisfaction. You can't break this.

### Part 14: The Black Box Review

#### Sunday, June 20, 2004 — 14:15 PM The Living Room

The laughter had died down. The ice cream was put away. Aunt Linda stood in the center of the living room, her hands clasped in front of her. She looked at the sprawling pile of happiness on the sofa, but her expression was serious.

"Alright," Aunt Linda announced. "I need the room."

The girls looked up.

"Laundry Brigade + Twins," Linda commanded. "Head to the Underworld. I need to speak to the Triad. Adults only."

Harper groaned, clinging to Freddie’s shirt. "But I just got back on! I’m comfortable!"

"Harper," Linda said, her voice dropping that specific octave that meant business. "Basement. Now."

Sarah and Jenna stood up immediately. They recognized the tone. This wasn't a punishment; it was a debriefing. They grabbed Amanda and Elara.

"Come on," Sarah said, pulling Harper up by her arm. "Let's go play cards."

Harper pouted, her lower lip trembling slightly as she looked at Freddie, but she let go. She trudged after her sisters. The basement door clicked shut.

Silence descended on the living room.

Linda pulled the ottoman over to the sofa and sat down, so she was knee-to-knee with Freddie.

Freddie was still in the center of the sofa. Paige was clamped to his left side. Jessica had moved from the love seat to his right side, taking the space Jenna had vacated. They sandwiched him.

"So," Linda said softly, looking Freddie in the eye. "We’ve done the food. We’ve done the laundry. We’ve done the distraction."

She leaned forward.

"Talk to me, Freddie. Really talk to me. What did she do to you?"

Freddie felt the air pressure in the room drop. His heart rate spiked. Warning: Accessing Corrupted Files.

He felt Paige’s hand tighten on his bicep. He felt Jessica’s hand cover his on his knee.

"She... reconfigured the narrative," Freddie whispered, staring at his hands.

"How?" Linda asked.

"She told me I was a parasite," Freddie said. The word hung in the air, ugly and clinical. "She said that my reliance on the Unit was preventing Jessica and Paige from executing their primary functions."

He took a shaky breath.

"She said Jessica wanted to move to New York," Freddie continued, his voice trembling. "To work in fashion. To be successful. She said I was the reason Jessica was stuck in 'muddy Virginia.'"

Jessica squeezed his hand so hard her knuckles turned white. "She lied, Freddie. I hate New York. It smells like garbage and anxiety."

"She said Paige wanted Italy," Freddie went on, tears pricking his eyes. "To study Art History. To live in a villa. She said Paige was only staying here... out of pity. Because I couldn't function alone."

"I have never wanted to go to Italy without you," Paige hissed, her voice furious. "Never."

"I know," Freddie choked out. "I know that now. But then... the data felt real. She isolated me. She repeated the input until it became truth."

He looked up at Linda. His glasses were fogging up.

"She made me write a note," Freddie confessed. A tear escaped, tracking down his cheek. "A suicide note. She dictated it. She told me to write that I was 'setting them free.' That I was deleting myself so they could live."

Linda’s face went pale. She brought a hand to her mouth.

"I was going to do it," Freddie whispered, his body starting to shake. "I was on the bus. I was going to wait until we got to Seattle, sign the papers she wanted... and then terminate the connection. I thought... I thought I was saving them."

The shaking became violent. The "Pasco Memory" tried to surface—the cold, the fear, the emptiness.

"Ground him," Jessica ordered sharply.

It happened instantly.

Paige threw her leg over his, pinning him to the sofa. She buried her face in his neck. "You are here. You are here. You are here."

Jessica wrapped both arms around his chest from the other side, crushing him between them. "Overwrite, Freddie. Overwrite the data. We came for you."

"You came," Freddie sobbed, collapsing into them. "You flew to Seattle. You found me."

"We will always find you," Paige promised fiercely. "There is no distance we won't travel."

"We turned it into a vacation," Freddie gasped, struggling to regulate his breathing. "We went to the Sky-Spire. We ate fish. We overwrote the bad data with good data."

"Yes," Jessica whispered into his ear. "We fixed it."

"I am never leaving," Freddie vowed, his voice breaking. "I am never leaving the Unit again. I cannot survive the silence."

They held him for a long time. The only sound in the room was Freddie’s ragged breathing and the steady hum of the air conditioner.

Finally, the shaking stopped. The pressure of his sisters—the sheer physical weight of their love—forced the panic back into its box.

Aunt Linda reached out. She placed her hand gently on Freddie’s knee, right on top of Jessica’s hand.

"She was a monster, Freddie," Linda said, her voice shaking with suppressed rage. "What she did... that wasn't love. That was torture."

She looked at the three of them. She saw how they were tangled together. She saw how Paige was practically sitting in his lap, how Jessica was shielding him.

She knew what the world would say. She knew what psychologists might say about "enmeshment."

"I know you three sleep together," Linda said suddenly.

Freddie froze. Paige tensed up, ready to fight.

"I saw the bed in the Master room," Linda continued calmly. "I saw the pillows. And I know you've been doing it since you got back."

She looked Freddie dead in the eye.

"Does it stop the nightmares?" Linda asked.

"Yes," Freddie whispered. "When I feel them... the terror cannot manifest."

"Does it make you feel safe?" Linda asked.

"Yes," Freddie said. "It is the only time the system fully powers down."

Linda nodded. She sat back on the ottoman.

"Then to hell with anyone who has a problem with it," Linda declared.

Paige blinked. "Really?"

"Really," Linda said firmly. "After what you've been through? After almost losing him? If sleeping in a puppy pile keeps him here, on this earth, safe and sound... then you buy a bigger bed if you have to."

She smiled, a sad, weary smile.

"Ellen would hate it," Linda noted. "Which makes me like it even more."

Freddie let out a wet, shaky laugh. He wiped his eyes on his shoulder.

"Thank you, Aunt Linda," Freddie said.

"Don't thank me," Linda said, standing up and smoothing her skirt. "Just promise me one thing."

"Anything," Freddie said.

"Don't you ever believe you're a burden," Linda said, pointing a finger at him. "You aren't the anchor holding them down, Freddie. You're the gravity holding them together. Look at them."

Freddie looked. He saw Paige, fierce and protective. He saw Jessica, strong and steady.

"Without you," Linda said, "they drift away. You keep them home."

She walked to the basement door.

"Now," Linda said, her voice lightening. "I'm going to release the prisoners before Harper claws through the drywall. Are you good?"

Freddie took a deep breath. He felt the weight of his sisters. He felt the truth of Linda’s words.

"Status," Freddie whispered.

"Green," Paige answered.

"Green," Jessica answered.

"Optimal," Freddie confirmed.

### Part 15: Clearance Denied

#### Sunday, June 20, 2004 — 14:20 PM The Underworld (Basement)

The door at the top of the stairs clicked shut, sealing the younger sisters in the concrete bunker.

Sarah sighed and sat down on her air mattress, shuffling a deck of worn playing cards. Jenna cracked open another soda, leaning against the dryer. Amanda and Elara were on the floor, building a tower out of plastic cups they had found in the pantry.

But Harper didn't sit down.

She stood at the bottom of the wooden staircase, staring up at the door. Her hand gripped the banister.

"Come on, Harper," Sarah called out, dealing the cards onto the sleeping bag. "Crazy Eights. You're in."

"I don't want to play," Harper whispered. She took a step up. The wood creaked.

"Harper, stop," Jenna warned. "Aunt Linda said to stay down here."

"I just want to listen," Harper said, taking another step. "I want to know what they're saying. Why did they make us leave? We're part of the Unit too."

"It's adult business," Sarah said, flipping a card. "That means boring stuff. Taxes. Insurance. The divorce."

"It's not taxes," Harper insisted. She climbed two more steps, putting her ear close to the gap under the door.

For a moment, there was only the low rumble of voices—Aunt Linda’s calm tone, Freddie’s soft murmur.

Then, the sound changed.

It wasn't a word. It was a sound Harper had never heard before. It was a ragged, high-pitched gasp. Then a sob. A deep, wrenching sound that vibrated through the floorboards.

Harper froze. Her eyes went wide.

"He's crying," Harper whispered, her voice trembling. "Freddie is crying."

On the floor, Amanda stopped stacking cups. She looked up at the ceiling, her expression darkening. Elara frowned, dropping a red cup.

"He never cries," Harper said, panic rising in her chest. "He didn't cry when he fell. He didn't cry when Mom yelled. Why is he crying?"

Another sob filtered down—a sound of pure, raw heartbreak.

"I have to go," Harper decided.

She scrambled up the stairs, her feet thumping on the wood. "I have to fix it! He needs a cuddle!"

"Harper, no!"

Sarah moved fast. She vaulted off the air mattress and sprinted to the stairs. Jenna was right behind her.

Harper reached for the doorknob.

"Let me out!" Harper yelled. "He's sad! I can help!"

Sarah grabbed Harper’s ankle through the railing. Jenna grabbed the back of her shirt. They hauled her backward.

"No!" Harper kicked, tears springing to her own eyes. "Let go! He needs me! I fit in his lap! I can make it better!"

"You can't go up there!" Sarah hissed, wrapping her arms around Harper’s waist and pulling her down to the concrete floor. "Aunt Linda said stay out!"

"But he's hurt!" Harper sobbed, struggling against Sarah’s grip. "Listen to him! He sounds broken!"

"He is broken!" Jenna snapped, her voice cracking slightly as she helped pin Harper down. "That's why they're talking! They're fixing him!"

"I can fix him!" Harper screamed.

"No, you can't!" Sarah shouted, finally wrestling Harper into a sitting position on the bottom step. "You're eleven, Harper! You can't fix this!"

The basement went silent, except for Harper’s heavy breathing and the muffled sounds of Freddie weeping upstairs.

Sarah took a deep breath, adjusting her glasses. She looked at Harper, whose face was red and wet with tears.

"Listen to me," Sarah said, her voice shaking but firm. "Freddie has... bad data. From the bad girl. From Vanessa."

Harper sniffled. "The one he ran away from?"

"Yes," Sarah said. "She put bad things in his head. And now... he has to get them out. And getting them out hurts. It's like... it's like popping a blister. It hurts before it heals."

"But why can't I hold him?" Harper whined, wiping her nose on her sleeve. "He likes it when I hold him."

"Because right now," Jenna said softly, sitting down next to them, "he needs the Triad. He needs Paige and Jessica and Aunt Linda. They have... higher clearance."

"Clearance?" Harper asked small voice.

"Yeah," Sarah nodded, using Freddie’s word. "Level 1 Clearance. We are Level 2. We handle the laundry. We handle the cuddles after. But the Triad handles the heavy stuff."

Harper looked at the top of the stairs. She could still hear the faint sounds of comfort—Jessica’s voice low and soothing, Paige’s fierce whispering.

"Is he going to be okay?" Harper whispered.

Amanda walked over. She stood in front of Harper. She reached out and patted Harper’s head, then pointed to the ceiling and gave a thumbs up.

"Amanda says yes," Elara translated from the floor. "She says the bad noise is stopping."

Harper listened. The sobbing had quieted. It was replaced by a steady, low murmur. The storm had passed.

"Okay," Harper whispered. She leaned her head on Sarah’s shoulder. "I hate Level 2."

"Me too," Sarah admitted, wrapping an arm around her little sister. "Being a kid sucks. You don't know anything."

"But we know he's safe," Jenna reminded them. "He's upstairs. He's not on a bus."

"Yeah," Harper sighed. She looked at the cards scattered on the sleeping bag. "Can I be the dealer?"

"Sure," Sarah said, standing up and pulling Harper with her. "But if you cheat, I'm telling Freddie."

"I never cheat," Harper lied, wiping her eyes.

They went back to the circle. They played cards in the cool, grey light of the Underworld, waiting for the signal that the adults were done and the Captain was ready for his Satellites again.

#### Sunday, June 20, 2004 — 14:35 PM The Underworld (Basement)

The card table was a blue sleeping bag pulled taut over the concrete floor. The five sisters sat cross-legged around it in a circle.

Sarah shuffled the deck. She did it the way Freddie did—a perfect bridge shuffle that made a satisfying thwip-thwip-thwip sound.

"Game is Crazy Eights," Sarah announced, dealing five cards to each player. "Eights are wild. Twos are pickup-two. Queens are skip. Acetaminophen rules."

"It's 'Aces High,' not 'Acetaminophen,'" Jenna corrected, picking up her hand and fanning it out. "You spend too much time reading Freddie's medical dictionaries."

"Standard rules apply," Sarah ignored her. "And since we have five players, we are using two decks mixed together. The draw pile is heavy."

Harper picked up her cards. She held them close to her chest, shielding them from Jenna’s prying eyes.

She looked at her hand: Two Hearts, a King of Spades, a Five of Diamonds, a Seven of Clubs, and... the Eight of Hearts. The magic card.

Usually, this was the part where Harper would slide the Seven of Clubs under her leg. Or maybe she would cough and accidentally-on-purpose drop a bad card into the discard pile when no one was looking. Cheating was her way of leveling the playing field against the older, smarter twins.

She glanced at Jenna. Jenna was distracted, trying to crack her knuckles.

Harper’s thumb hovered over the Seven. She could drop it. It would be so easy.

Then she looked up at the ceiling.

It was quiet upstairs now. The crying had stopped. Freddie was recovering.

She remembered Sarah’s threat. If you cheat, I'm telling Freddie.

Harper imagined Freddie’s face. Not angry—he never really got angry. But disappointed. She imagined him looking at her with those sad eyes behind his glasses and saying, “Your data is corrupt, Harper.”

She didn't want to be corrupt. She wanted to be Level 1. She wanted to be worthy of the hug she was going to get when the door opened.

Harper tightened her grip on the cards. She pushed the Seven back into the fan.

I’m playing clean, she told herself. I’m going to win for real.

"Play commences," Sarah stated. She flipped the top card: Four of Diamonds.

"Boom," Jenna said, slapping down a Four of Spades. "Match."

Amanda and Elara (playing as a single team) whispered to each other. Elara nodded. Amanda placed a Four of Hearts on the pile.

"Stop changing the suit!" Jenna groaned. "I was hoarding Spades!"

It was Harper’s turn. The suit was Hearts. She had a Two of Hearts.

"Pickup two, Sarah," Harper said, slapping the card down.

Sarah glared at her over her glasses. "Aggressive."

Sarah drew two cards. "Fine. My turn."

The game moved fast. Cards slapped against the sleeping bag. The tension in the basement shifted from "scared listening" to "competitive focus."

Five minutes later, it was down to the wire.

Jenna had one card left. She was grinning, bouncing her leg. "UNO! Wait, wrong game. Last card!"

"Don't get cocky," Sarah muttered, checking her own hand of three cards.

The twins played a Queen.

"Skip Jenna!" Elara shouted.

"Hey!" Jenna yelled. "That's sabotage!"

"Strategy," Elara smirked. Amanda high-fived her.

The play passed Jenna. It came to Harper.

Harper looked at her hand. She had the Eight of Hearts. The wild card.

The current pile was a Queen of Diamonds.

Harper looked at Jenna, who was clutching her single, winning card (which Harper was 90% sure was a Spade).

If Harper played the Eight, she could change the suit to anything. She could change it to Hearts. Jenna didn't have Hearts. Jenna would have to draw.

Harper played the card.

"Eight," Harper announced clearly. "Suit is Hearts."

Jenna’s jaw dropped. She looked at her last card (the King of Spades). She looked at Harper.

"You little..." Jenna narrowed her eyes. "You peeked! You saw my hand!"

"I didn't!" Harper shot back, sitting up straight. "I didn't look! I just guessed because you were complaining about Hearts earlier!"

Jenna looked at Sarah. "She cheated. Lieutenant, execute her."

Sarah paused. She looked at Harper. She studied Harper’s face—the defiant chin, the wide, honest eyes, the hands placed flat on the sleeping bag where everyone could see them.

Sarah had been watching. Harper hadn't dropped a card. She hadn't leaned over. She had played a perfect, clean game.

"Negative," Sarah ruled. "Her play was valid. Logic dictates you were hoarding Spades. Harper intercepted the pattern."

Sarah smiled a tiny, proud smile.

"Good kill, Harper."

Harper beamed. She felt lighter than air. She beat Jenna, and she didn't have to hide anything.

"Draw, Jenna," Harper commanded, pointing to the deck.

Jenna groaned and snatched a card from the pile. "I hate this game. I hate Level 2."

Upstairs, the floorboards creaked. Heavy footsteps moved toward the basement door.

The lock clicked. The door opened.

"Coast is clear!" Aunt Linda’s voice called down, sounding cheerful and relieved. "Who wants ice cream?"

Harper threw her cards into the air—ruining the game instantly.

"ME!" she screamed.

She scrambled up, abandoning the victory for the prize she really wanted. She bolted up the stairs, past Aunt Linda, and ran straight into the living room.

Freddie was still sitting on the sofa, sandwiched between Paige and Jessica. His eyes were red, and he looked tired, but when he saw Harper, he opened his arms.

"Come to port," Freddie whispered.

Harper launched herself at him. She climbed into his lap and buried her face in his neck, smelling the soap and the salt of his tears.

"I didn't cheat," Harper whispered fiercely into his ear. "I played honest. I promise."

Freddie held her tight, resting his chin on her head.

"I believe you," Freddie said. "Good girl."

#### Sunday, June 20, 2004 — 14:40 PM The Living Room

The force of Harper’s hug knocked the wind out of him, but Freddie didn't pull away. He adjusted his grip, wrapping his arms around her waist to secure her. She was trembling against him, her face pressed so hard into his shoulder that he could feel the dampness of her tears soaking through his flannel shirt.

Paige reached out and rubbed Harper’s back. Jessica leaned in, resting her chin on Freddie’s shoulder, watching the youngest sister with a soft, sad expression.

"I heard you," Harper whispered into his neck, her voice thick with mucus and emotion. "I heard you through the floor. It sounded like you were hurting."

Freddie took a steadying breath. He didn't try to lie. He didn't try to say 'I wasn't crying' or 'It was just allergies.' Harper was smart. She dealt in facts, just like him.

"I was hurting," Freddie admitted, his voice low and rumbling against her ear. "But it was a necessary hurt. Like resetting a bone."

Harper pulled back just an inch. She looked at him. Her eyes were red, but they were sharp. She scanned his face, looking for the cracks.

"It was her, wasn't it?" Harper said. It wasn't a question. "The girl from the porch. Vanessa."

Freddie blinked. He looked at Aunt Linda, who was standing by the kitchen door. Linda gave a small, barely perceptible nod. She knows.

"Yes," Freddie said. "She introduced... significant errors into my system."

"She tricked you," Harper stated firmly. She wiped her nose with the back of her hand. "She made you think we didn't want you. That's why you went on the bus."

Freddie felt a pang in his chest. He hadn't realized how much she had pieced together from the whispers and the tension.

"She did," Freddie whispered. "I thought I was... taking up too much space. I thought you would be better off without the extra weight."

Harper frowned. Her eyebrows scrunched together in a way that looked exactly like Jessica.

"That's stupid," Harper declared.

Freddie let out a tiny, surprised huff of laughter. "Is it?"

"Yeah," Harper said. "You're the Captain. The satellites fall out of orbit without the planet. You can't just leave."

She reached up and touched his cheek, her fingers sticky from the card game but warm.

"Besides," Harper added, her voice dropping to a conspiratorial whisper. "Jessica and Paige went and got you. They flew on a plane. That means you're worth a lot of money."

"They rescued the asset," Freddie agreed, glancing at his twin. Paige smiled, squeezing his arm.

"So you're fixed now?" Harper asked, looking deep into his eyes behind his glasses. "The bad thoughts are gone?"

"The data has been overwritten," Freddie promised. "I told Aunt Linda everything. We purged the system. I am empty of the bad stuff."

He squeezed her tighter.

"And now I am refilling the space with good stuff. Like you."

Harper relaxed. Her shoulders dropped. The panic that had driven her up the stairs evaporated, replaced by the heavy, sleepy comfort of being exactly where she wanted to be.

"Good," Harper murmured, resting her head back on his shoulder. "Don't let her back in. She's banned."

"Banned for life," Jessica confirmed from his right side.

The basement door opened again. Sarah, Jenna, Amanda, and Elara filed out. They saw Harper safe in Freddie’s lap. They saw Freddie’s dry eyes. They saw the calm returning to the room.

"Status?" Sarah asked, adjusting her glasses.

"Stable," Freddie announced.

"Excellent," Aunt Linda clapped her hands together, breaking the heavy mood. "Because I believe I promised ice cream. And I have a gallon of Mountain-Peak Vanilla and a gallon of Fudge-Swirl melting on the counter."

"Ice cream!" Elara cheered.

Harper shifted on Freddie’s lap. She didn't get down.

"Can I eat mine here?" Harper asked. "I don't want to move."

Freddie looked at Jessica. Jessica shrugged, smiling. "It’s a flannel shirt, Mike. It washes."

"Affirmative," Freddie said to Harper. "You remain at port. I will request a delivery."

"I'll get it," Paige said, standing up and pressing a kiss to the top of Freddie’s head. "One bowl of fudge swirl, coming up."

Freddie sat back against the beige cushions. He held Harper. He listened to his sisters arguing over bowls in the kitchen. He felt the phantom weight of the suicide note dissolving in his memory, replaced by the very real weight of an eleven-year-old girl who refused to let him go.

He was home.

## Chapter 11: The Big One-Five

### Part 1: Trajectory Calculations

#### Monday, June 21, 2004 — 14:00 PM 105 Pepper Street SE

The house was buzzing. The twins were officially fifteen.

In the kitchen, Sarah was demonstrating her "Suffocation Protocol" on a lit match over the sink, just to show off to Harper. She licked her fingers, pinched the flame, and grinned as the smoke curled up.

"See?" Sarah said. "Physics. The moisture absorbs the thermal energy."

Freddie and Paige, sitting at the kitchen table, recoiled in unison. They both pulled their hands into their sleeves.

"Negative," Freddie said, shaking his head. "That is thermal insanity. I require my nerve endings."

"Agreed," Paige shivered, leaning against him. "I prefer blowing. Or water. Touching fire is a glitch."

"It’s efficient!" Sarah argued.

"It’s terrifying," Jenna countered, walking in. "Okay, enough science class. Jessica said we’re going out. Where are we going?"

Jessica walked in, twirling the keys to the sedan. Aunt Linda jingled the keys to the minivan.

"Since you are now fifteen," Jessica announced, "and technically adults-in-training, we decided against the kiddie pizza palace. We are going to Galaxy Lanes."

"Bowling?" Jenna pumped her fist. "Yes! I’m gonna crush you, Sarah."

"We shall see," Sarah said coolly, adjusting her glasses. "Bowling is just geometry with heavy spheres."

Freddie hesitated. He looked down at his wheelchair. He looked at his hands, which weren't strong enough to swing a ten-pound ball.

"I will... observe," Freddie suggested. "I can keep score."

"Nope," Jessica grinned. "You’re playing, Captain. They have technology."

### Part 2: The Ramp

#### 14:45 PM Galaxy Lanes, Christiansburg

The bowling alley smelled of floor wax, rental shoes, and greasy fries. It was a cacophony of crashing pins, arcade bells, and synth-pop music.

To **Freddie**, it was chaos. But it was good chaos. It was the sound of people having fun, not the sound of people fighting.

They secured two lanes at the end of the row.

**Jessica** walked up to the control console. She looked at the blinking green screen.

"Okay," Jessica announced to the group. "Since we have players ranging from age nine to... well, Aunt Linda... and since we are here to celebrate, not to suffer, I am activating the **Safety Protocol**."

She pressed a button. Bumper Rails: ON.

HISSS-CLACK.

Down the length of both Lane 1 and Lane 2, long black rubber rails rose up from the gutters. They snapped into place, effectively sealing off the trench on either side.

"Bumpers!" **Harper** cheered, jumping up and down. "Pinball bowling!"

"Hey!" **Jenna** protested, crossing her arms. "I don't need bumpers. I'm fifteen."

"You threw three gutter balls in a row last time," **Sarah** reminded her, adjusting her glasses. "The data supports the bumpers."

"Besides," **Aunt Linda** laughed, lacing up her shoes. "It makes the game faster. No one likes watching a ball roll into the sewer."

**Freddie** rolled his wheelchair up to the approach on Lane 2. He looked at the long wooden lane. With the bumpers up, it looked less like a challenge and more like a chute. A safe path.

"I approve," Freddie stated, looking at the black rails. "It is a containment field. The error margin is reduced to zero."

"Exactly," Jessica grinned. "You literally can't miss. Now, let’s get you set up."

She dragged over the metal ramp—**The Launcher**.

"Okay, Captain," **Paige** said, grabbing a six-pound orange ball. "You have the ramp. You have the bumpers. Do your worst."

Freddie leaned forward. He studied the geometry.

Without the bumpers, he would have had to aim for a perfect center line. But with the bumpers... he could get creative.

"Angle the ramp ten degrees to the right," Freddie instructed.

"Right?" Jessica asked. "That points at the wall."

"Trust the ricochet," Freddie said.

Jessica shifted the ramp. Freddie placed the orange ball at the top. He held it with one hand. He waited for the lane to clear.

"Launch," Freddie whispered.

He let go.

The ball rumbled down the metal ramp—clank-clank-clank. It hit the wood and rolled slowly toward the right side.

"It’s gonna hit the rail," Jenna critiqued from the next lane.

THUMP.

The ball hit the right bumper. It bounced off the rubber at a sharp angle, redirecting toward the left.

THUMP.

It hit the left bumper. It zig-zagged back toward the center.

It was a slow-motion pinball game. The ball wove back and forth, gathering no speed but maintaining its forward momentum.

It reached the pins. It hit the head pin on a perfect, glancing angle.

CRASH.

The pins toppled. Because the ball was moving slowly, they didn't explode; they fell over like dominos, knocking each other down in a lazy, satisfying cascade.

**STRIKE.**

The overhead monitor flashed a cheesy animation of a bowling pin dancing.

"Geometry!" Freddie announced, raising a fist.

"That was total luck!" Jenna yelled.

"Calculated trajectory," Freddie corrected. "The bumpers are part of the equation."

**The younger sisters took the lane next.**

**Amanda** and **Elara** (Lane 1) took full advantage of the rails. They didn't even try to aim. They just hurled the ball as hard as they could. Their balls bounced 4, 5, sometimes 6 times before hitting the pins.

PING-PONG-PING-PONG-CRASH.

They giggled maniacally every time it hit a rail.

Even **Harper** found her confidence. Usually, she was afraid of messing up. But with the rails up, she couldn't fail. She stepped up, closed her eyes, and bowled a spare.

**Paige** sat next to Freddie while Aunt Linda took her turn.

"You like the bumpers?" Paige asked, handing him a soda.

"Affirmative," Freddie said, watching the ball bounce safely down the lane. "It prevents the 'gutter' scenario. It keeps the object in play."

He looked at his sisters—laughing, high-fiving, safe within the lane.

"It is... a good system," Freddie decided. "We should keep the bumpers up."

"Don't worry," Paige smiled, squeezing his shoulder. "We are the bumpers. You aren't going in the gutter, Freddie. Not on our watch."

Freddie watched his orange ball return through the ball return machine.

Safe return, he thought. Always.

### Part 3: The Arcade Intervention

#### 16:00 PM

After two games (Freddie won the second one thanks to his "ramp precision"), the group migrated to the arcade section.

It was a flashy area filled with flashing lights and ticket munchers.

Sarah and Jenna went straight for the Rhythm-Mat-Mania machine. They were fifteen now; they had to prove their coordination. They stomped on the flashing arrows on the metal platform, laughing as they missed the beats of the techno music.

Harper took the twins (Amanda and Elara) to play Alley-Roller (the game where you roll wooden balls up a ramp into numbered rings).

Freddie and Paige rolled over to the Claw Machine.

It was filled with generic plush aliens.

"Remember the blue bear?" Paige asked, leaning on the joystick console next to him.

"Affirmative," Freddie said. "The Mall Rescue."

"We did good today," Paige said, watching Sarah and Jenna laughing on the dance floor. "They're happy. They aren't thinking about Mom and Dad."

"We successfully diverted the narrative," Freddie agreed.

Just then, a loud noise erupted from the Velocity-X racing game nearby. A kid lost his game and kicked the machine hard. BANG.

Freddie flinched. His hands flew up to cover his ears. The sudden, violent noise triggered a flash of the "bad data"—the shouting, the bus engine.

Paige reacted instantly. She didn't make a scene. She just stepped in front of him, blocking his view of the angry kid. She placed her hands on his hands (which were over his ears).

"Focus on me," Paige said. Her voice was calm. "Look at the aliens."

Freddie looked at the claw machine. He looked at Paige’s face.

"Green alien," Paige said. "Top left corner. Do you see it?"

Freddie focused. "Affirmative. Three eyes."

"Target acquired," Paige smiled. "Let’s get it."

She guided his hand to the joystick. She kept her other hand on his shoulder, grounding him.

"You steer," Paige said. "I'll hit the button."

Freddie took a breath. The loud kid was gone. The noise faded into the background. He focused on the claw.

He moved the stick. Whirrr. He adjusted the angle. Whirrr.

"Drop," Freddie whispered.

Paige slammed the red button.

The claw dropped. It grabbed the green alien by its bulbous head. It lifted. It held.

Plunk.

Freddie reached into the prize chute and pulled out the cheap, neon-green alien. He handed it to Paige.

"For the co-pilot," Freddie said.

Paige took it. She squeezed it, then she squeezed his hand.

"Thanks, Captain."

They turned back to watch their sisters. Sarah had just tripped over her own feet on the rhythm machine, and Jenna was laughing so hard she was crying. Aunt Linda was buying everyone sodas.

"Mission status?" Paige asked.

Freddie looked at the green alien in her hand, and the smile on Jenna’s face.

"Victory," Freddie said.

### Part 4: The Carbohydrate Loading

#### Monday, June 21, 2004 — 18:30 PM 105 Pepper Street SE

The convoy returned to Base. The minivan and the sedan pulled into the driveway, unloading nine tired, hungry, but victorious Averys. The adrenaline of the bowling alley was wearing off, replaced by the primal need for fuel.

Aunt Linda dropped her purse on the counter. "Okay, birthday girls. You have the conn. What is the menu?"

Jenna didn't hesitate. "I want grease. I want cheese. I want Tony’s."

Sarah adjusted her glasses. "Pizza is the most logical caloric distribution system for a group of this magnitude. It minimizes dishwashing protocols."

"Pizza it is," Jessica agreed, grabbing the cordless phone and the thick phone book. "But we have a conflict of interest. What are the toppings?"

This was the annual debate.

"I want the Garbage Truck," Jenna declared. "Sausage, pepperoni, onions, peppers, mushrooms, jalapenos. If it fell on the floor, I want it on the pie."

"Disgusting," Sarah wrinkled her nose. "I require structural integrity. I want the White Garden. Garlic sauce, spinach, chicken. No tomato interference."

"And the rest of the troops?" Jessica asked, looking at the younger ones.

"Plain cheese!" Harper, Amanda, and Elara shouted in unison.

"And for the Captain?" Jessica looked at Freddie, who was parked at the head of the table, with Paige already sitting next to him.

"Standard pepperoni," Freddie requested. "Predictable variables."

"Okay," Jessica dialed the number. "One Garbage Truck, one White Garden, two large Cheese, one Pepperoni. And... five liters of soda. God help us."

#### 19:15 PM

The doorbell rang.

The delivery driver was a teenager named Kyle. He stood on the porch holding five massive, steaming cardboard boxes. He looked terrified as the door opened and he was greeted not by an adult, but by a wall of excited girls.

"Pizza!" Elara screamed.

"Make a hole!" Jenna shouted, grabbing the top two boxes. "Hazardous materials coming through!"

They loaded the dining room table. It was a tight squeeze. The table was extended to its limit, but with nine people and five pizzas, there was barely room for elbows.

The Seating Chart:

- Head of Table: Freddie (The Core).

- Right Flank: Paige (The Anchor). She served Freddie his slice (Pepperoni), cutting it into strips for him so he didn't have to struggle with the floppy crust.

- Left Flank: Jessica (The Shield).

- Center Stage: Sarah and Jenna (The Birthday Girls).

- The Satellites: Harper, Amanda, Elara, and Aunt Linda filled in the gaps.

"A toast!" Aunt Linda announced, raising a red plastic cup of Citrus-Pop.

Everyone raised their cups (or juice boxes).

"To Sarah and Jenna," Linda smiled. "Fifteen. You survived middle school. You survived the move. You survived the... family drama."

"We survived the genes," Jenna grinned, taking a massive bite of her loaded pizza. A piece of sausage fell onto her plate. "Oops. Casualty."

"To the Unit," Sarah corrected, clinking her cup against Freddie’s. "Status: Operational."

"Operational," Freddie agreed, taking a bite of his pepperoni slice.

The room filled with the sounds of chewing, laughing, and the specific chaos of a large family dinner.

Jenna ate with enthusiasm, getting sauce on her chin. Sarah ate methodically, folding her thin-crust white pizza in half to prevent spillage. Harper tried to see how far she could stretch the cheese from her slice before it snapped (record: 14 inches).

Freddie sat in the middle of it all.

He watched Amanda steal a pepperoni off Elara’s plate. He watched Paige wipe a crumb off his own shirt before he even noticed it was there. He listened to Jenna describing her "epic" strike in frame 7 (conveniently forgetting the bumpers).

He felt full. Not just from the pizza, but from the noise.

"Freddie," Harper called out from the end of the table. "Do you like the pepperoni?"

"Affirmative," Freddie said. "The spice level is nominal. The crust is optimal."

"Good," Harper beamed. "Because we saved you the last piece."

Freddie looked at the decimated boxes. They were greasy cardboard skeletons now. But true to her word, in the center of the pepperoni box, one single slice remained, guarded by Harper like a treasure.

"Thank you, Harper," Freddie smiled.

#### 19:45 PM

The boxes were cleared. The table was wiped down (mostly).

"Okay," Jessica stood up, exchanging a quick, subtle glance with Aunt Linda. "The main course is demolished. Now, for the procedure."

"Procedure?" Jenna asked, burping quietly.

"The Cake Protocol," Jessica said seriously. "Now, listen. Linda and I discussed this. We know you guys wanted fifteen individual candles."

"Yeah," Jenna nodded. "One for every year of suffering."

"Well," Jessica sighed, shaking her head. "We did a risk assessment. Fifteen open flames in a small room with this many people wearing flannel? It’s a fire hazard. The insurance won't cover it."

Sarah pushed her glasses up. "That is a valid safety concern. The heat output of fifteen wicks could trigger the smoke detector. Furthermore, the melted wax distribution would ruin the icing integrity."

"Exactly," Jessica said, keeping her face perfectly neutral. "So, we compromised. We got the big number candles. A '1' and a '5'. It’s safer. Less fire. Easier to blow out."

"Boring," Jenna rolled her eyes. "But fine. Bring out the sugar."

"Prepare the table," Aunt Linda commanded. "Lights out!"

Harper ran to the light switch. The room plunged into darkness, save for the ambient street light filtering through the front window.

Jessica went into the kitchen. She struck a match.

Fzzzt.

She lit the large blue 1. She lit the large blue 5.

The wicks caught, and the numbers glowed with a steady yellow flame.

Jessica lifted the heavy sheet cake.

"Incoming!" Jessica sang out, walking the glowing cake into the dining room.

### Part 5: Pyrotechnic Redundancy

#### Monday, June 21, 2004 — 19:30 PM 105 Pepper Street SE

The dining table was a disaster zone of empty Tony’s Pizza boxes, crumpled napkins, and drained soda cans. Nine people had just consumed enough carbohydrates to fuel a small power plant.

The noise level was severe. Jenna was recounting a story about the drive up, gesturing wildly with a fork. Harper, Amanda, and Elara were vibrating with sugar energy. Aunt Linda was laughing loudly at something Jessica said.

At the head of the table, Freddie sat in his wheelchair. He was the eye of the hurricane. Paige was sitting on a dining chair next to him, but her body was angled entirely toward him, her arm hooked through his, her head resting on his shoulder. She was his physical anchor against the sonic waves.

"Status?" Paige murmured into his sleeve.

"Input is maximum," Freddie reported, his eyes scanning the chaotic table. "But the data is positive."

"Okay, clear the runway!" Jessica shouted.

She set the massive sheet cake down in the center of the debris field. The blue wax 1 and 5 burned with surprisingly intense, bright yellow flames, bridging the gap between Sarah’s neat vanilla side and Jenna’s chaotic chocolate side.

"Alright, no speeches," Jenna said, eyeing the frosting. "Let's just blow them out so we can eat."

They began the song. It was the loudest, most off-key rendition of "Happy Birthday" ever performed in Christiansburg. Freddie didn't sing, but he tapped his fingers on his armrest in perfect time with the rhythm, humming the bassline under his breath.

The song ended in messy cheering and applause.

"Make a wish!" Elara screamed.

Sarah and Jenna leaned in over the cake. They looked at each other.

"One, two, three!"

WHOOSH.

They blew hard. The two bright flames flickered, danced, and then vanished, leaving behind two thick trails of white smoke curling toward the ceiling lamp.

The room cheered. Linda started to reach for the cake server.

Freddie, watching closely from the end of the table, noticed an anomaly.

The wicks weren't glowing the usual dull red of dying cotton. They were sparking. Tiny, intense pinpricks of white light were dancing on the blackened tips.

Magnesium, Freddie’s internal database supplied. Pyrotechnic redundancy.

Crack. Hiss. FWOOSH.

Suddenly, the smoke trails ignited. The flames jumped back onto the wicks of the 1 and the 5, burning just as brightly as before.

The room fell silent.

"What the heck?" Jenna blinked.

"Did you miss?" Harper asked.

"We didn't miss," Sarah frowned, leaning closer. "Airflow was sufficient."

Jessica was biting her lip, trying desperately not to laugh. Aunt Linda was hiding her grin behind a napkin.

"Try again," Jessica suggested sweetly. "Maybe you didn't wish hard enough."

Sarah and Jenna leaned in again. They took deeper breaths.

WHOOOOOOSH.

They blew until their faces were red. The flames died again. Smoke billowed.

They waited one second. Two seconds.

Crack. Spark. FWOOSH.

The 1 and the 5 roared back to life.

"IT’S MAGIC!" Amanda silently screamed, clapping her hands wildly.

"They’re rebooting!" Harper yelled.

Jenna glared at the candles. It was personal now.

"Oh, you want to play?" Jenna growled. She stood up, leaning over the table, and started actively chopping at the smoke with her hand while blowing short, furious puffs of air. "Die! Die! Die!"

Sarah took a tactical approach. She pursed her lips into a tight O shape, aiming a laser-focused stream of air at the base of the 1.

They went out.

They relit.

They went out again.

They relit again, faster this time.

Freddie watched the chaos with a calm fascination. He loved the physics of it. He loved the sheer stubbornness of the chemistry.

"Self-repairing systems," Freddie murmured to Paige. "Highly efficient."

"Jessica is evil," Paige whispered back, giggling against his shoulder.

Finally, Sarah realized wind wasn't the answer.

"Suffocation protocol!" Sarah commanded.

She licked her thumb and forefinger and pinched the wick of the 1. At the same time, Jenna grabbed a glob of chocolate icing and dunked the wick of the 5 into it.

The flames died with a final hiss. They did not relight.

The room erupted in laughter. Jessica finally cracked, doubling over in her chair.

"Happy birthday, suckers!" Jessica gasped. "Trick candles. The guy at the party store said they were 'relentless.'"

"You’re the worst sister ever," Jenna said, licking chocolate icing off her fingers, grinning ear to ear.

"Ingenious mechanism," Sarah admitted, wiping soot from her fingers onto a napkin. "Magnesium-infused wicks. Well played."

Aunt Linda turned the lights back on. The room was bright, messy, and full of the people Freddie loved most in the world.

He looked at the cake, scarred by the battle with the unkillable candles. He looked at Sarah and Jenna, laughing as they cut the first slices.

He felt Paige squeeze his arm, grounding him in the moment.

Vanessa had offered him silence. She had offered him a life where nothing ever went wrong because nothing ever happened.

But this—this loud, messy, resilient chaos, where even the candles refused to quit—this was his life.

"System status," Freddie whispered to himself, watching Amanda take a massive bite of blue icing.

Optimal.

### Part 6: The Voltage Spike

#### Monday, June 21, 2004 — 20:15 PM 105 Pepper Street SE

The cake was decimated. The blue numbers were smoking ruins on the platter.

Jessica and Aunt Linda, wise to the ways of glucose, had carefully sliced thin, reasonable pieces for Amanda and Elara. They were happily coloring on the floor, content and stable.

But the Birthday Girls (Sarah and Jenna) and the opportunist (Harper) had gone rogue. They had consumed massive corner pieces laden with thick buttercream roses.

The result was immediate and terrifying.

At the center of the room, Freddie was in his safe mode. He was docked on the sofa, with Paige heavily leaned against his left side. Her head was on his shoulder, her arm looped through his, her leg pressed against his leg. She was warm, heavy, and still. She was the anchor that kept the room from spinning.

Then, the anomaly occurred.

Harper was vibrating. Literally vibrating.

She was standing in the middle of the living room, bouncing on the balls of her feet. Her pupils were dilated. She had a smear of blue icing on her chin that looked like war paint.

"I’m not tired," Harper announced, her words coming out like bullets from a machine gun. "I could run to the bowling alley. I bet I could run there right now. Do we have a jump rope? I need to jump."

"Harper, sit down," Aunt Linda suggested from the kitchen.

"I can't sit!" Harper yelled happily. "My legs are full of bees!"

She swiveled her head. She locked eyes with Freddie.

To Harper, Freddie was the Core. He was the designated Cuddle Station.

"Cuddle!" Harper shouted.

She launched herself at the sofa.

"Incoming!" Paige warned, bracing herself.

Harper scrambled onto Freddie’s lap. Usually, this was a docking maneuver he welcomed. He liked the weight of her. He liked the quiet way she would rest her head on his chest.

But this wasn't a dock. It was a collision.

Harper tried to settle in, but her body wouldn't cooperate. She was a live wire. She twitched. She kicked her legs. She drummed her fingers on his chest in a manic, irregular rhythm. She buried her face in his neck, breathing fast and hot, then immediately popped up to look around the room.

Freddie flinched. His hands hovered over her waist, unsure of where to grab.

The sensation was wrong. It wasn't the deep, steady pressure he needed. It was erratic. It was sharp. It was like holding a bag of popcorn while it was popping.

"Negative," Freddie said, his voice tight. He pressed back into the sofa, trying to retreat into Paige’s stillness.

"Harper," Freddie said, trying to hold her still, but she slipped through his grip like a ferret. "You are oscillating. I cannot ground you."

"I know!" Harper giggled, squirming. She poked his nose. She poked his glasses. "I'm buzzing. Can you feel it? It feels like sparkles!"

"Input is too high," Freddie said, squeezing his eyes shut. The erratic movement was disrupting his equilibrium. "Paige, assist."

"She's a hazard," Paige laughed, but she saw the tension in Freddie’s jaw. "Harper, eject. You're too hot for the Captain right now. You're scrambling his sensors."

"I have too much voltage!" Harper agreed happily.

She realized she couldn't stay still long enough to enjoy the hug anyway. Her skin felt too tight for her body.

"Okay, gotta go!" Harper declared.

She slid off Freddie’s lap, leaving his shirt rumpled and his glasses crooked.

Freddie immediately grabbed Paige’s hand and pulled it tight against his chest, needing to reset the baseline.

"Status?" Paige asked, smoothing his shirt.

"Frequency mismatch," Freddie exhaled, leaning his head back on Paige’s shoulder. "She is operating on AC current. I require DC."

Harper began doing laps around the coffee table, making race car noises.

Meanwhile, the twins were experiencing their own manic episode.

Jenna was on the floor, wrestling with a sofa cushion. She was laughing hysterically at nothing. "The pillow is fighting back! It’s got me! Send reinforcements!"

Sarah, usually the calm Lieutenant, was pacing the room, gesturing wildly with a napkin she was shredding into confetti.

"Did you know magnesium burns at 3,100 degrees?" Sarah lectured rapidly to no one in particular, walking in tight circles. "That's hot enough to melt iron. We ate cake next to melting iron. That is statistically unsafe but awesome. Do we have any iron? We should test it."

"Status check?" Jessica asked, leaning in the kitchen doorway with a trash bag, watching the asylum.

"System overload," Freddie reported, watching Harper blur past him for the fifth time. "Meltdown imminent."

"Ten minutes," Jessica predicted, checking her watch. "Then we crash."

### Part 7: The Shutdown Sequence

#### Monday, June 21, 2004 — 21:15 PM 105 Pepper Street SE

The crash was absolute.

One minute, the living room was a mosh pit of vibrating sisters. The next, it was a field of casualties.

Harper was lying face-down on the carpet, one arm draped over a footstool. Jenna was slumped against the armchair, blinking in slow motion, her mouth slightly open. Sarah was staring blankly at a knot in the wood floor, her glasses sliding down her nose.

Jessica stood over them, hands on her hips.

"Alright, zombies," Jessica announced, clapping her hands once. "We are not sleeping in sticky clothes. Hygiene protocol is active. Teeth and Toilet. Move out."

Harper groaned into the carpet. "Can't move. Bones are melted."

"If you don't move, I'll carry you to the hose in the backyard," Jessica threatened affectionately.

Aunt Linda emerged from the hallway. "I’m taking the hallway bathroom to get my makeup off. It’s occupied for ten minutes."

The hallway bathroom was the only "official" bathroom on the main floor. With Linda inside, that created a bottleneck.

Amanda and Elara (the responsible ones who hadn't overdosed on sugar) stood by the doorway holding their toothbrushes.

"We can wait," Elara said.

Jenna, however, had no patience left. She peeled herself off the armchair.

"I’m not waiting," Jenna slurred. She grabbed her toothbrush from the side table (where she had left it earlier for some reason). "I’m going to the Underworld."

"Me too," Sarah sighed, standing up like an old woman. "Efficiency."

"Wait for me," Harper mumbled, crawling upright.

Freddie watched them shuffle toward the basement door. He stayed firmly planted on the sofa, Paige still resting against his shoulder.

"I will remain at Command," Freddie stated clearly.

He knew the layout. The basement was an open zone. Now that the "Laundry Brigade" had broken the seal on the Pittsburgh Toilet, the girls were treating it like a normal facility.

But Freddie operated on a strict code of chivalry and logic. He was nineteen. They were his younger sisters. And there were no walls down there.

"You aren't going down?" Paige asked, stifling a yawn.

"Negative," Freddie said, adjusting his glasses. "That is currently a restricted zone. I will respect the perimeter."

Paige smiled, squeezing his arm. "Good Captain."

#### The Underworld (Basement)

Down in the cool concrete bunker, the "Sugar Crash Squad" went to work.

There was no modesty, only tiredness.

Sarah turned on the faucet of the deep utility sink. It groaned and spat cold water. She and Jenna stood side-by-side, brushing their teeth over the paint-stained drain.

"Move over," Jenna grumbled, spitting foam. "You're hogging the water."

"You have a massive head," Sarah retorted weakly, rinsing her brush.

In the corner, Harper didn't even hesitate. She walked over to the Pittsburgh Toilet—the lonely porcelain throne sitting right next to the furnace.

She pulled down her pajama pants and sat down.

A day ago, she would have died of embarrassment. She would have held it until her bladder exploded rather than go in front of her sisters.

But now? Sarah was spitting toothpaste three feet away. Jenna was splashing water on her face. The dryer was humming.

Harper just sat there, swinging her legs, staring at the exposed pipes on the ceiling.

"Did you see the look on Freddie's face when I tried to hug him?" Harper asked, her voice echoing slightly in the open room. "He looked like he was holding a bomb."

"You were a bomb," Sarah said, wiping her mouth on her sleeve. "You were oscillating at a frequency incompatible with his sensory processing."

"I was just happy," Harper yawned.

She finished, stood up, and flushed. KA-WHOOSH. The sound was deafening in the concrete box.

"My turn," Jenna said, handing Sarah her toothbrush to hold.

Jenna swapped places with Harper. Harper went to the sink to brush her teeth, standing next to Sarah.

They rotated through the stations—sink, toilet, sink—with the efficiency of a pit crew. No doors. No locks. No secrets. Just sisters getting ready for bed in the only way that worked for a family this size.

### Part 8: The Photographic Evidence

#### Monday, June 21, 2004 — 21:30 PM The Living Room

The "Hygiene Squad" had returned from the basement, and Amanda and Elara had descended from the upstairs bathroom. The living room was now filled with eight clean, minty-smelling siblings wearing mismatched flannel pajamas.

Jenna and Sarah were heading toward the basement door to retrieve the air mattresses for the "Mega-Bed" construction.

"Hold it," Aunt Linda commanded, standing in the center of the room.

She was holding a silver and black camera—an Opti-View 35mm—which she had dug out of her purse.

"Before we turn this room into a campground," Linda announced, winding the film wheel with a satisfying crk-crk-crk sound. "I need evidence."

"Aunt Linda," Jessica groaned, rubbing her eyes. "We look like train wrecks. I have pizza grease in my pores."

"I don't care," Linda said firmly. "Everyone on the sofa. Now."

"All of us?" Harper asked, yawning. "It’s a three-person sofa."

"It’s a family sofa," Linda corrected. "We improvise. Freddie, stay put. You are the anchor."

Freddie, still parked in the center of the beige sofa, adjusted his glasses. "I am ready to receive docking procedures."

Linda began directing traffic like an air traffic controller.

"Okay, Triad in the middle," Linda ordered. "Paige, port side. Jessica, starboard side. Squeeze him in. I want no daylight between you."

Paige snuggled into Freddie’s left, resting her head on his shoulder. Jessica wedged herself into his right, wrapping an arm around his waist.

"Okay, now the layers," Linda pointed. "Harper, you are cleared for landing. Low voltage this time."

Harper, significantly calmer now that the sugar had left her system, climbed up. She didn't bounce. She didn't vibrate. She simply curled up on Freddie’s lap, tucking her legs in and resting her head on his chest.

"Acceptable," Freddie whispered, resting his chin on the top of her head. "Heart rate is nominal."

"Twins, up and over," Linda gestured.

Amanda and Elara climbed up the back of the sofa. They draped themselves over the top cushions, their arms hanging down over Freddie’s shoulders, their chins resting near his ears. Freddie was now wearing a hat made of sisters.

"Birthday Girls, flank positions," Linda directed.

Sarah and Jenna, too big to fit on the cushions without crushing someone, sat on the floor in front of the sofa, leaning back against Freddie’s knees. Sarah rested her cheek against his left leg; Jenna rested hers against his right.

Linda stepped back. She looked through the viewfinder.

It was a mountain of flannel, hair, and limbs. You couldn't tell where one sibling started and another ended. In the center of it all, Freddie looked small, weighed down, and overwhelmed.

And he looked happier than she had ever seen him.

The terror of the last week—the bus, the note, the empty wheelchair in the hallway—felt a million miles away.

"Squeeze in!" Linda called out, her voice thick with emotion. "Smile!"

"Cheese!" Elara yelled.

"Pizza!" Jenna corrected.

Whiiiiiiine-CLICK.

The flash blinded them for a second.

"One more," Linda insisted, winding the film. "Just in case someone blinked."

Whiiiiiiine-CLICK.

"Okay, now a silly one," Linda laughed, wiping a tear from her eye. "Go nuts."

Jenna stuck her tongue out. Elara made bunny ears behind Freddie’s head. Harper crossed her eyes. Jessica puffed out her cheeks. Freddie simply pushed his glasses down his nose and looked over the rim with a "stern librarian" face.

Whiiiiiiine-CLICK.

Linda lowered the camera. She looked at the tableau one last time before breaking the spell.

She thought about Vanessa. She thought about how hard that girl had tried to make Freddie feel like a burden—like his needs were too heavy for anyone to carry.

Look at this, Linda thought fiercely. He isn't a burden. He's the foundation.

"Alright," Linda sniffled, putting the camera back in her purse. "Photo session adjourned. Laundry Brigade, go get the mattresses. Let's get this camp set up."

As the pile slowly disentangled itself—limbs retracting, sisters sliding off—Freddie stayed still for a moment, savoring the lingering warmth of the pressure.

"System check," he whispered to Paige as she sat up.

"Full battery," Paige smiled, squeezing his hand. "We're full."

"Affirmative," Freddie said.

He watched Sarah and Jenna drag the deflated air mattresses up from the basement, ready to build the raft that would keep them all together through the night.

### Part 9: The Platoon Deployment

#### Monday, June 21, 2004 — 21:45 PM The Living Room

The photo session was over. The evidence was secured in Aunt Linda's camera. Now, the logistics began.

Aunt Linda clapped her hands. "Alright, listen up. Tomorrow morning, the minivan leaves for Virginia Beach at 0900 hours. The invasion force is retreating."

A collective groan went up from the younger siblings. They didn't want to leave.

"But," Linda continued, smiling. "For tonight, we are not separating. No one is going to the basement. We are doing a full-scale camp-out right here."

Jessica took charge. This was their house—hers, Paige’s, and Freddie’s. They had bought it from Great Aunt Marge before she passed, using the settlement money. It was their fortress, and tonight, they were lowering the drawbridge.

"Operation Mega-Bed is a go," Jessica announced. "Sarah, Jenna—haul the air mattresses up from the Underworld. Harper, help me move the coffee table."

#### The Heavy Lifting

The heavy oak coffee table was slid all the way against the TV stand, opening up a vast rectangle of beige carpet.

Then, Jessica turned to the sofa.

"Evacuate the cushions," Jessica commanded.

She and Paige pulled the cushions off. Jessica gripped the metal handle hidden inside the frame and pulled.

CREAAAK-CLUNK.

The mechanism unfolded. The beige sofa transformed into a Queen-sized sleeper bed with a thin but acceptable mattress.

"The Command Module," Freddie noted, looking at the bed. "Capacity: Three."

"Exactly," Paige said, smoothing a fitted sheet over the mattress. "You're in the middle, Captain. We're the bumpers."

#### The Barracks

Sarah and Jenna returned from the basement, dragging bundles of vinyl. The electric pumps roared to life—VRRRRRRRRR—filling the living room with the sound of inflation.

One by one, the "Platoon" took shape on the floor.

#### The Bedding Assignments:

- Command Module (Sofa Bed):

  - Freddie transferred from his wheelchair to the center of the Queen bed. He lay back against the pillows, finally taking the weight off his spine.

  - Paige climbed in on his left (Port Side).

  - Jessica climbed in on his right (Starboard Side). It was a tight squeeze for three adults, but they fit like puzzle pieces—a solid wall of Averys.

- The Perimeter (The Floor):

  - Sarah and Jenna took two twin air mattresses on the far side of the room, near the window.

  - Amanda and Elara shared a double air mattress near the kitchen entrance.

  - Aunt Linda claimed a twin mattress near the armchair, acting as the overseer.

That left one spot.

Harper stood holding her pillow, looking at the layout. There was one twin air mattress left.

"I want that one," Harper pointed.

She dragged her mattress until it was jammed right up against the metal frame of the sofa bed, directly below where Freddie was lying.

"Harper, you're going to be underfoot," Sarah warned.

"I don't care," Harper said, dropping her pillow. "I need to be near the Captain. In case of... turbulence."

Freddie looked over the edge of the sofa bed. He could see Harper looking up at him, her eyes wide and tired.

"Permission granted," Freddie whispered.

#### 22:15 PM

"Lights out," Aunt Linda whispered from her corner.

She clicked the lamp off. The room plunged into darkness, lit only by the faint green glow of the VCR clock: 10:15.

The room settled. The rustle of sleeping bags and the creak of the vinyl mattresses slowly faded.

It was a full house. A house that Freddie, Paige, and Jessica owned. A house that was usually quiet, now filled with the breathing of nine people.

"Freddie?" Harper’s whisper came from the floor, inches from the bed frame.

"I am here, Harper," Freddie murmured into the dark.

"Are you okay?" Harper asked. "Are you squished?"

Freddie felt Paige’s arm draped over his chest. He felt Jessica’s shoulder pressed against his. He felt the solid presence of his sisters on all sides.

"I am contained," Freddie whispered back. "It is optimal."

"Okay," Harper yawned. "Goodnight, Captain."

"Goodnight, Harper."

Freddie closed his eyes. The nightmares couldn't get in here. There was no room for them. The bed was full. The floor was full. The house was full.

For the first time since the bus ride, Freddie Avery slept without dreaming.

### Part 10: The Decompression Chamber

#### Tuesday, June 22, 2004 — 08:30 AM 105 Pepper Street SE

The sun streamed through the living room blinds, illuminating the wreckage of the sleepover.
